content:
I Compared Disk Sizes Across 5 Design Systems Building the Same Dashboard. The Numbers Didn’t Make Sense at First.

4 min read
4 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

System Design Benchmark ScreenshotsI wasn’t planning to write this one. I was just cleaning up after the WebSocket benchmark — closing tabs, deleting test projects — when I noticed something on the way out: the folder sizes.
Same screen. Same fake stock API. Same chart, same layout, same five identical financial dashboards I’d already benchmarked for render performance. Ant Design, Chakra UI, Material Design, Mantine, and my own Logical Brutalism, all running side by side in five browser tabs.
I right-clicked each project folder out of habit, expecting nothing interesting. Here’s what du -sh gave me:

• Ant Design: 246 MB

• Mantine: 234 MB

• Chakra UI: 192 MB

• Material Design: 191 MB

• Logical Brutalism: 43 KB
I stared at that last number for a solid ten seconds assuming I’d pointed the command at the wrong folder.
First Instinct: This Has to Be node_modules
It was. Obviously. Four of these projects ship a full React component library, its peer dependencies, its peer dependencies’ peer dependencies, and whatever build tooling each framework drags in to make development workflow nice. node_modules is always the elephant in the room, and I almost stopped the investigation right there — "yeah, node_modules is huge, everybody knows that, not a real finding."
But that felt like giving up one layer too early. The real question wasn’t “why is node_modules big” — everyone already knows the answer to that. The real question was: what happens to each project once you strip node_modules out and look at what’s actually yours?
So I deleted it from all four and remeasured.
The Second Number Was the Actual Surprise
With node_modules gone — just source files, configs, public assets, whatever each scaffolding tool generated on day one:

• Ant Design: ~60 KB

• Chakra UI: ~60 KB

• Material Design: ~60 KB

• Mantine: 807 KB (the outlier — heavier scaffolding, more generated boilerplate out of the box)

• Logical Brutalism: still 43 KB
This is the part that actually rewired how I think about this comparison. Without dependencies, three of the four frameworks land in roughly the same ballpark as Logical Brutalism — 60KB of source code is 60KB of source code, regardless of which component library you’re importing from. The code I wrote isn’t smaller than the code anyone else wrote. That’s not the story.
The story is that Logical Brutalism’s number didn’t change. At all. Because there was no node_modules to delete in the first place.
Why There’s Nothing to Delete
Ant, Chakra, Material, and Mantine each require a runtime component library installed locally — the actual JavaScript that renders their buttons, cards, and grid systems has to live in node_modules for the app to run. Most also pull in a build pipeline on top of that: Next.js or Vite, a CSS-in-JS engine, type definitions, transitive dependencies three and four layers deep that you never chose and will never look at.
Logical Brutalism doesn’t have a component library to install, because the “library” is a static token layer — CSS variables and utility classes compiled once, not a JavaScript runtime that needs to be present on disk to function. There’s no .next folder because there's no Next.js build step in this setup. No dist folder full of transpiled component code. The project is the source code, full stop. Nothing gets installed because nothing was depended on in the first place.
That’s a different category of comparison than “smaller bundle.” A smaller bundle is still the same kind of thing as a bigger bundle, just optimized. Zero dependencies isn’t an optimized version of having dependencies — it’s not playing that game at all.
What This Actually Costs You
I want to be straight about the trade-off here, because “zero dependencies” sounds like a free lunch and it isn’t one.
Every convenience those libraries hand you — a date picker that handles every locale and edge case, a table component with sorting and virtualization built in, theme tooling with twenty configuration options — has to be either built by hand in a zero-dependency setup, or it simply doesn’t exist yet. I’m not shipping a feature-complete component library. I’m shipping the seven components this specific dashboard needed, written for this specific dashboard.
For a large team building a sprawling internal tool with forty screens and a deadline next month, that trade-off probably doesn’t make sense — you want Ant Design’s 200+ pre-built components and you’re happy to pay 246MB of disk and a slower npm install for it. This finding isn't an argument that everyone should delete their dependencies tomorrow.
It’s an argument that the cost is real and almost nobody measures it, because node_modules has become invisible. It's just there. Nobody opens a terminal and runs du -sh on their own project out of curiosity, because the answer is assumed before the command even runs.
The Actual Takeaway
Five identical dashboards. Same API, same chart, same layout. One of them needed 43KB to exist. The other four needed somewhere between 191MB and 246MB to run the exact same screen, and once you remove the part that was never really “their code” to begin with, three of them turn out to be roughly the same size as the zero-dependency version anyway.
The lesson I took from this isn’t “frameworks are bloated, write everything from scratch.” It’s narrower and more useful than that: disk footprint isn’t a proxy for how much code a team wrote. It’s a proxy for how many decisions a team outsourced to a dependency tree they’ll never fully read. Sometimes that’s the right call. It’s just worth knowing you’re making it, instead of finding out by accident while deleting old test folders.
What does not resolve, does not exist.
